coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots from eggshell membrane ashes by using sodium hydroxide and their usage for degradation of methylene blue
coredata.dc:creator: Jusuf, Bella Nathanael
Sambudi, Nonni Soraya
Isnaeni,
Samsuri, Shafirah
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (C-dots) is a recent member of carbonaceous nanomaterial with particle size below 10 nm, equipped with unique fluorescent properties under UV light irradiation. Many methods for the production of C-dots have been reported by introducing carbon-rich waste. In this work, a facile and rapid method was developed to synthesize C-dots from the ashes of eggshell membrane (ESM) in sodium hydroxide solution assisted by one-step microwave irradiation. The optimum time in preparing the C-dots and the highest photoluminescence intensity are achieved for 1 min and by using 1 mol L−1 NaOH, respectively. The resulting C-dots with an average size of 3.88 ± 0.56 nm exhibited pH and NaOH solvent-dependent fluorescent behaviour in the visible light range. They are found to be able to degrade methylene blue under sunlight irradiation, with increasing degradation ranging from 26% to 43% for 5 ppm to 1 ppm concentration of methylene blue.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
pH sensor
Eggshell membrane
Methylene blue degradation

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special Issue on Sustainable Solutions in Chemical Engineering

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence intensity of C-dots synthesized from 3 mg of ESM at 1 mol L−1 NaOH solution assisted by microwave treatment for 20 s, 1 min and 2 min.

TEM image of C-dots in (a), (b) NaOH 1 mol L−1 solution (red circles show vague lattice fringe of atoms forming C-dots), and (c), (d) water (red circle shows the groups of C-dots). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
SAED patterns of C-dots in (a) NaOH 1 mol L−1 solution, and (b) water.
FTIR spectra of C-dots synthesized in (a) NaOH 1 mol L−1 solution, and (b) water.
UV–vis spectra of C-dots in NaOH (a) with and without C-dots, and (b) with different concentrations of NaOH. (c) Band gap estimations of C-dots in NaOH from absorption spectra.

Photoluminescence of C-dots solutions with different NaOH concentrations: (a) 1 M, (b) 10−1 M, (c) 10−2 M, (d) 10−3 M, and (e) 10−4 M. (f) Intensity of fluorescence with different pH. and (g) Photoluminescence spectra with different concentration of NaOH.
(a) Photoluminescence spectra, (b) UV–vis spectra, and (c) Band gap estimations of different concentration of ESM in water.
(a) Photocatalytic degradation of different concentrations of MB under solar irradiation, (b) kinetic model of degradation, and (c) degradation comparison between MB solution with and without C-dots.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots from eggshell membrane ashes by using sodium hydroxide and their usage for degradation of methylene blue

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Surya University, Tangerang 15810, Indonesia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Universiti Teknologi PETRONAS, Perak 32610, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Physics, Indonesian Institute of Sciences (LIPI), Kawasan Puspiptek, Serpong 15314, Indonesia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (C-dots) is a recent member of carbonaceous nanomaterial with particle size below 10 nm, equipped with unique fluorescent properties under UV light irradiation. Many methods for the production of C-dots have been reported by introducing carbon-rich waste. In this work, a facile and rapid method was developed to synthesize C-dots from the ashes of eggshell membrane (ESM) in sodium hydroxide solution assisted by one-step microwave irradiation. The optimum time in preparing the C-dots and the highest photoluminescence intensity are achieved for 1 min and by using 1 mol L−1 NaOH, respectively. The resulting C-dots with an average size of 3.88 ± 0.56 nm exhibited pH and NaOH solvent-dependent fluorescent behaviour in the visible light range. They are found to be able to degrade methylene blue under sunlight irradiation, with increasing degradation ranging from 26% to 43% for 5 ppm to 1 ppm concentration of methylene blue.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQD, C-dots or Carbon dots) is one of the new carbon materials which its particle size is below 10 nm. It is firstly discovered in 2004 through a purification process of one dimensional carbon nanotube with electrophoresis method [1]. C-dots have gained a lot of attention since then. Several synthetic methods have been developed including top-down method such as laser ablation, arc discharge, plasma-induced fabrication, and bottom-up method such as hydrothermal, pyrolysis, microwave treatment, and chemical oxidation [2]. It is found that C-dots possess many advantages such as good solubility in various solvents, non-toxic, photo-stable, sensitive and selective on analyte [3]. Thus, recent researches on applications of C-dots have merged in several fields, such as chemical sensor, photocatalysts, solar cell, biosensor, and bio-imaging [2,4]. Many experiments on C-dots synthesis have been conducted by utilizing carbon-rich resources, such as egg whites [5], pine tannin [6], banana juice [7], orange juice [8], lignin [9], and ESM [10].

One of unique properties of C-dots is its ability to exhibit visible fluorescence under UV light irradiation [11]. All of the publications over C-dots have reported various optical and chemical properties depending on different compositions, surface passivation, and surface trap. Most of the publications conducted were to study the surface trap mechanism by varying carbon sources, chemical reagents, and pH. In this study, C-dots were synthesized according to a facile method using microwave irradiation with the use of ESM waste. Two main modifications have been achieved such as lowering the drying temperature of carbon materials and varying the pH of alkaline solution to synthesize carbon dots. Microwave irradiation is a low cost, rapid and facile method to synthesis carbon dots [2]. However, poor control size is still a problem [2]. One of approaches in overcoming it is by using suitable solvent to help synthesizing homogeneous carbon dots size. In this study, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was used to control the size and improve the luminescence of carbon dots.

ESM has been gaining many interests as material for bio-template for the synthesis of nanoparticles, sorbent of heavy metals, organics, the main component of biosensors, the material applicable in medicine and others. It consists of fibrous structure between eggshell and egg white [12]. In particular, ESM was selected as carbon source due to its low cost and availability. Compared to previous work [10], our approach successfully exhibits low pre-treatment temperature at 200 °C, short synthesis time (one minute), and utilization of household wastes. Particularly, C-dots dispersed in different pH alkaline solution display a pH-dependent emission and absorption spectra which potentially could serve as a pH probe sensor.

The ability of C-dots for photocatalytic process is also shown in this study. C-dots and nanoparticles have been used solely for photocatalytic process from previous studies [13–15]. C-dots could absorb the extension of light spectrum from UV Vis to near infra-red [16]. The photoexcitations in C-dots are responsible for fluorescence emission and also reduction oxidation process from the associated electrons and holes available for photocatalytic process [16]. The NaOH solution (from 10−4 mol L−1 to 1 mol L−1) and water were used to synthesis the C-dots under microwave irradiation time of 20 s to 2 min. The degradation of methylene blue was performed for the concentration of 1 ppm to 20 ppm. The synthesized C-dots in this study shows good performance for degradation of methylene blue under sunlight irradiation.
Eggshell membrane was peeled off from the eggshell waste of fresh broiler chicken obtained from local market. NaOH, and methylene blue were purchased from Merck Chemical Co. (Germany).

In this study, C-dots were produced by following the synthesis steps of carbon dots by using microwave irradiation method [10]. At first, ESM was peeled off, and then rinsed with distilled water to remove impurities and albumen residue. The cleaned ESM was dried in laboratory drying oven at 200 °C for 4 h and crushed to form ESM powder by using domestic blender. Then, ESM powder was sieved through 60-mesh wire sieve to obtain homogeneous particle size. The NaOH solutions with concentration at 10−4, 10−3, 10−2, and 10−1 mol L−1 were prepared via serial dilution from NaOH stock solution. Afterwards, 3 mg of ESM powder was added to 5 ml of NaOH solution (10−4–1.0 mol L-1) and irradiated in a domestic microwave oven (560 W) with time variation of 20 s, 1 min, and 2 min at ambient temperature Synthesis of C-dots in water was performed at the optimum microwave irradiation time based on the synthesis of C-dots in NaOH solution by using 3 mg of ESM powder at ambient temperature. The final products of C-dots synthesized in NaOH solution and water were centrifuged at 12.000 rpm for 10 min to remove the unreacted ESM powder residue.

The absorption spectra of C-dots were recorded at room temperature using Libra S12 UV–vis spectrophotometer (UK) with wavelength ranging from 200 to 700 nm and an interval of 5 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by using Ocean Optics USB2000 spectrophotometer (USA) with a 405 nm laser as the excitation source. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were measured on Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS-10 spectrometer (USA) with wavelength ranging from 450 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging of C-dots and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) were carried out on a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN (USA). The measurement of size distribution of C-dots was performed by counting over 50 particles using Nano Measurer software.

The degradation of methylene blue by C-dots was carried out under direct sunlight on early June during the dry season at city of Tangerang Selatan, outskirt of Jakarta (GPS coordinates: 6°15′58.7″S 106°37′19.2″E) from 8 AM to 6 PM. The ambient temperature is between 26 °C and 31 °C. The average daily irradiation of sunlight in Jakarta is around 4.8 kWh m−2 [17]. The recorded irradiation of sun in Jakarta on June started at 7AM to 6PM with average irradiation on 7AM, 8AM, 9AM, 10AM, 11AM, 12 PM, 1PM, 2PM, 3PM, 4PM, 5PM, and 6 M at around 4.4, 16.7, 33.4, 47.8, 57.9, 63.6, 62.4, 52.7, 41.5, 30.1, 15–8, 3.9 gr Cal cm−2, respectively [18]. The methylene blue solutions with concentrations of 1 ppm, 2 ppm, 3 ppm, 4 ppm, 5 ppm, 10 ppm, 15 ppm, and 20 ppm were prepared. The volume ratio of methylene blue and C-dots in water was set at 1:4. An aqueous solution of methylene blue was taken in glass vessel, and the degradation of methylene blue was monitored with the UV Vis spectrophotometer at time intervals of 0, 1, 2, 5, and 10 h. The% degradation of methylene blue was calculated by using the formula:
C 0
− C
C 0
× 100 %
Where C 0 is the initial concentration, and C is the concentration after sunlight irradiation at the time interval.

C-dots samples with microwave irradiation time ranging at 20 s, 1 min, and 2 min show decreasing in volume from each initial volume of 5 ml to approximately 0,5–1,75 ml. After microwave treatment, all samples experience colour change from transparent solution into yellowish colloids.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the fluorescence intensity increased gradually as the microwave irradiation time changes from 20 s to 1 min for samples with constant concentration. The fluorescence intensity decreased after 2 min of microwave irradiation which indicated a turn-off phenomenon [10,19–21]. The synthesis of C-dots from ESM ashes can be explained by using 3 steps, which consists of (1) breaking of ESM into smaller fragments, (2) polymerization and growing of C-dots, and (3) passivation of C-dots surface [10]. These mechanisms support the improved fluorescence intensity by applying longer microwave irradiation time, which will produce more carbon nanoparticles in a sample. However, when the fluorescence intensity decreased when the microwave irradiation time increased, this could relate to turn-off phenomenon, which indicates that the formed grains experience a surface damage [10]. Therefore, the 1 min microwave irradiation time is the optimum condition for the synthesis of C-dots in this research. Furthermore, the synthesis of C-dots was performed with different concentrations of NaOH, which constitutes to different pH values.

Fig. 2 shows the morphology and size of C-dots synthesized from ESM in 1 mol L−1 NaOH with pH of 12.49 (Fig. 2a, b) and in water with pH of 6.6 (Fig. 2c, d) by using TEM analysis. C-dots in NaOH solution are spherical with average diameter of 3.88 ± 0.56 nm and size ranging from 2.5 nm to 5 nm. In contrast, C-dots synthesized in water forms agglomerates of irregular shape with the average size of 4.46 ± 0.77 nm. According to Kong et al., OH- from NaOH contributes to the formation of protective shell which causes the effective diameter of C-dots to becomes smaller [22]. Moreover, the TEM analysis also shows vaguely lattice fringe of atoms forming C-dots grains (Fig. 2b).

In Fig. 3 , the set of d-values from SAED pattern of C-dots in NaOH are estimated at 0.239 nm, 0.208 nm, 0.147 nm, 0.127 nm, and 0.104 nm, while SAED pattern of C-dots in water display a set of d-values of 0.568 nm, 0.242 nm, 0.208 nm, 0.194 nm, 0.181 nm, and 0.118 nm. In accordance with study by De et al. [7], SAED pattern containing a radius of more than 3.3 Å (0.33 nm) indicates an imperfect crystallisation process as found in C-dots sample in water (0.568 nm). Based on these results, the C-dots formed in NaOH solution show smaller lattice spacing compared to the ones synthesized by using water as solvent. According to Jia et al. [23], lattice space with 0.208 nm spacing observed in the crystalline structure of the as-prepared C-dots, which is consistent with both (103) diffraction plane of diamond-like (sp3) carbon and (102) diffraction plane of sp2 graphitic carbon.

Fig. 4 shows the comparison of FTIR spectra of C-dots in 1 mol L−1 of NaOH solution and water. Based on FTIR measurement, it is found that the resulting C-dots particles synthesized in NaOH and water have similar functional groups. The peaks at 3331 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 for C-dots in NaOH correspond to the stretching mode of OH and CO, respectively. These two functional groups were also revealed at 3332 cm−1 and 1636 cm−1 for C-dots in water. Analysis of OH and CO functional groups in FTIR spectra are supported by several studies at 3384 cm−1 and 1691 cm−1 [23], and 3431 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 [13]. While OH group can be indicated from water molecules, CO group comes from eggshell membrane molecule composition. According to Park et al. and Wang et al., raw eggshell membrane contains some functional groups such as, OH, NH2, CH, CO, NH, CN, CC, CO, and CS [24,25]. Every functional group owns its average bond energy which indicates how much energy is needed to break atomic bonding. The average bond energy of some functional groups such as OH, CH, CO, NH, CN, CC, CO, dan CS are 467 kJ/mol, 413 kJ/mol, 745 kJ/mol, 391 kJ/mol, 305 kJ/mol, 614 kJ/mol, 358 kJ/mol, and 259 kJ/mol, respectively [26]. From these data, the CO group bonding has the highest average bond energy among other functional groups in ESM, which is unable to be broken after one minute synthesis by using microwave irradiation, and drying treatment at 200 °C. In contrast, Wang et al. reported that instead of CO group, COO group was formed in their experiment with drying temperature of 400 °C and 5 min of microwave irradiation, where the reaction condition may support the formation of COO bond [10].

As can be seen on Fig. 5 a, the absorption peak of C-dots in NaOH 1 mol L−1 is 2.5 times higher compared to pure NaOH 1 mol L−1. The region in between shows that C-dots are responsive to UV light with wavelength ranging from 200 to 400 nm by absorbing a larger quantity of photon energy. Moreover, the absorption spectra of C-dots in different NaOH concentration are also observed in Fig. 5b. The UV–vis spectra of C-dots show typical optical absorption in the UV region with a tail extending to the visible range. The appearance of a shoulder peak at 240 nm can be due to electronic transition of π → π* of CC bonds, and an intensive peak at around 258 nm can be due to transition of n → π* of CO bonds [27–37]. From these data, the absorbance of C-dots in this study is mainly in UV light spectrum, with small coverage in visible light spectrum. It is found that the fluorescence intensity improves along with increased NaOH solution and reaches its maximum in 1 mol L−1 of NaOH. The band gap values of C-dots are estimated by plotting hv vs (αhv)1/2 and drawing tangents, where α is the absorption coefficient, and hv is the photon energy [38]. The band gap values of C-dots in 10−4 M, 10−3 M, 10−2 M, 10−1 M, and 1 M of NaOH are 2.7 eV, 2.95 eV, 2.9 eV, 3 eV, and 2.85 eV, respectively (Fig. 5c).

Based on Fig. 6 , it is found that photoluminescence spectra encounter red shifts to longer wavelength from 469.3 nm to 525.42 nm. This red shift phenomenon occurs along with increasing of pH from 8.87 to 11.13. Additionally, there is a blue shifting phenomenon discovered in C-dots samples with pH ranging from 11.13 to 12.49. The origin of redshift occurs along with increase in pH, which shows that this can be due to the gradual creation of C-dots in the medium that absorbs and emit light at longer wavelength [39]. According to Choudhury, the blue shift occurs under high pH conditions due to pH induced deprotonation effect that produces the negatively charged C-dots in the medium [39]. According to Kong et al., the higher the concentration of OH-passivates the defects on C-dots, and creates a shell protective shell [22]. This shell protection contributes to the decrease in diameter of C-dots effectively, so that the wavelength shifts to shorter length based on quantum confinement theory.

The photoluminescence spectra show increasing in intensity by the increasing of concentration of NaOH, which means that at high pH the photoluminescence is brighter (Fig. 6). Previous studies by Ratnesh et al. and Wang et al. show the same agreement with these photoluminescence results, for the synthesis of CdTe and C-dots, respectively [10,40]. These results can be caused by more favourable affinity of quantum dots due to the deprotonation of phenolic functional group. Previous study by Choudhury et al. which shows the mechanistic insights on pH effect on luminescence of C-dots, explains that at high pH the nature of energy levels could be modified, and consequently influence the electronics transition in C-dots [39]. At low pH, the H-bonding effects lead to strong vibrational coupling of OH functional groups with sp2 carbon nano-domains, hence broad band of absorption spectra appeared [39]. The H-bonding effect disappears at the high pH, where the vibrational coupling between states is reduced, and energy levels become more discrete. Therefore, absorption band is more pronounced since electronic transitions are more sharply defined [39].

The solution of C-dots in water was used for this experiment because the degradation of methylene blue can be caused by caustic environment solely. Therefore, the usage of NaOH solution was avoided. In this experiment, 15 mg of ESM C-dots is used for the degradation of methylene blue, due to its highest photoluminescence spectrum compared to the other amounts of ESM (Fig. 7 ). The increasing of photoluminescence intensity is due to the increasing amount of C-dots formed when the amount of ESM as source was also increased. The band gap of 15 mg of ESM C-dots is estimated at 2.8 eV, while 10 mg ESM, 7 mg ESM and 5 mg ESM are estimated at 2.9 eV and 3.0 eV, respectively (Fig. 7c). C-dots are known to possess energy gap ranging from UV to near Infrared wavelength, hence it is able to harvest solar light energy for photocatalytic process [41,42]. Previous studies by Li et al., Wang et al. and Bhati et al. show the photocatalytic ability of C-dots under UV and visible light irradiation for the degradation of methylene blue [13–15].

As shown in Fig. 8 , the degradation of methylene blue increased gradually with increasing of time. For various concentration of methylene blue, the effect of C-dots upon degradation can be seen to decrease by the increasing concentration of methylene blue. The degradation of methylene blue of 40% to 65% was achieved after 1 h for the concentration of 5 ppm to 1 ppm. On the other hand, 10 ppm to 20 ppm experienced the decrease in degradation from 35% to 10% after 1 h. After 5 h of irradiation, more than 70% of methylene blue degraded for the concentration of 1 ppm to 5 ppm, and more than 90% of degradation achieved after 10 h of irradiation. This phenomenon indicates that photon energy of direct sunlight has caused C-dots to generate active oxygen radicals such as
O2 and
OH which attack methylene blue, as proposed by previous researches in using C-dots synthesized from Citrus grandis, N-doped C-dots, and magnetic C-dots to degrade methylene blue under sunlight irradiation [43–45]. The higher concentration of methylene blue resulted in longer time for degradation, where after 10 h the degradation was recorded at 65%, 50%, and 35% for 10 ppm, 15 ppm, and 20 ppm, respectively. This indicates that the usage of C-dots to degrade the methylene blue under solar light irradiation could achieve more than 50% degradation for the concentration of methylene blue under 15 ppm in this study.

The C-dots can improve the degradation of methylene blue up to 43% in comparison to methylene blue degradation under solar irradiation without C-dots (Fig. 8c). The improvement of methylene blue degradation for each concentration was found to be 43%, 39.5%, 36%, 31%, 26%, and 14% for the concentration of 1 ppm, 2 ppm, 3 ppm, 4 ppm, 5 ppm, and 10 ppm respectively. Above 10 ppm the degradation of methylene blue was found to be similar to the degradation of methylene blue without using C-dots. Therefore, under solar light irradiation C-dots performs well in degrading methylene blue with the concentration up to 10 ppm. The kinetic model of methylene blue degradation fits the first order of reaction kinetic with R-squared values of 0.9605, 0.9722, 0.9778, 0.981, 0.9864, 0.913, 0.9358, and 0.9591 for the concentration of methylene blue of 1 ppm, 2 ppm, 3 ppm, 4 ppm, 5 ppm, 10 ppm, 15 ppm, and 20 ppm, respectively (Fig. 8b). In accordance with the data published by Parangtopo et al., the intensity of solar radiation increased until 5 h after the experiment of methylene blue degradation started, and the decreasing of solar radiation can be observed after 6 h of experiment [18]. In this study, the degradation of methylene blue is highly increased especially during 5 h since experiment started which ranging from 30% to 84%. The increasing of degradation shows similar agreement with the increasing of solar radiation intensity, and similar results have been shown by Sun et al. in degrading the methylene blue using Sn-doped ZnO under sunlight irradiation for 10 h [46].

The synthesis of C-dots from ESM has been successfully performed at 1 min under microwave irradiation with NaOH solution at concentration of 1 mol L−1 as the optimum condition. The synthesized C-dots have spherical morphology with around 3.88 nm in diameter. The photoluminescence of C-dots can be controlled by varying pH of solution by using different concentration of NaOH. The increasing photoluminescence intensity was found by the increasing pH of solution. The photocatalytic ability of C-dots was performed by degradation of methylene blue under sunlight irradiation. The C-dots perform well in degrading low concentration of methylene blue. Therefore, the synthesized C-dots have the ability to be utilized to degrade low concentration of pollutant under sunlight irradiation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Short Term Research Fund (STIRF) 0153AA-F89 project funded by Universiti Teknologi PETRONAS and TWAS Research Grant (17-208 RG/MSN/AS_C − FR3240300071).
